
## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: How a Cumulonimbus Forms

Since cumulonimbus is the most important vertical cloud for hydrology, the following flowchart shows its thermodynamic and dynamic life cycle as a **block-level functional architecture**.

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[Input Surface Heat and Moisture] --> S2[Convective Lifting and Parcel Ascent]
    S2 --> S3[Stage 1 Cumulus Stage updraft dominates]
    S3 --> S4[Stage 2 Mature Stage Updraft and Downdraft coexist]
    S4 --> S5[Stage 3 Dissipating Stage Downdraft dominates anvil remains]
    S5 --> S6[Output Rain Hail Lightning and Flood Runoff]

    S1 --> P1[Check Buoyancy CAPE]
    P1 --> P2{Convective Available Potential Energy sufficient}
    P2 --> P3{Yes triggering mechanism present}
    P3 --> S2
    P2 -- No --> S7[Output Fair Weather cumulus only no storm]
    P3 -- No --> S7

    S4 --> H1[Hail formation zone between minus 10C and minus 30C]
    H1 --> H2[Output hailstone growth by accretion]
    S4 --> L1[Lightning generation charge separation]
    L1 --> L2[Output cloud to ground and intra cloud strokes]

    style S2 fill:#FFE4B5
    style S3 fill:#FFDAB9
    style S4 fill:#FF8C00
    style S5 fill:#A9A9A9
    style S6 fill:#4682B4
```

## 4.3 Altitude-Pressure Reference Schematic (for Numerical Problems)

```mermaid

    A0[Mean Sea Level 1013.25 hPa 25C standard] --> A1[Low Cloud Layer 0 to 2000 m 1013 to 795 hPa]
    A1 --> A2[Middle Cloud Layer 2000 to 6000 m 795 to 472 hPa]
    A2 --> A3[High Cloud Layer 6000 to 12000 m 472 to 194 hPa]
    A3 --> A4[Tropopause Boundary Above 12000 m below 194 hPa]

    A1 --> T1[St Sc Ns Cb base]
    A2 --> T2[As Ac]
    A3 --> T3[Ci Cs Cc]

    style A0 fill:#90EE90

    style T3 fill:#191970
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Read this diagram from bottom to top:** The pressure drops roughly **exponentially** with height; each cloud family occupies a specific pressure range that corresponds to its temperature regime (and therefore its phase — water vs. ice).

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define clouds. Explain the role of condensation nuclei in cloud formation.
**[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Module 1, 3 marks]**

**Model Answer:**

> A **cloud** is a visible aggregate of minute water droplets or ice crystals suspended in the atmosphere, formed when moist air is cooled to its **dew point** and water vapour condenses around microscopic particles called **condensation nuclei** (CCN).

> **Role of Condensation Nuclei (3 key points expected):**
> 1. **Lower the energy barrier:** Pure water requires extreme supersaturation (RH > 400%) to condense spontaneously. Hygroscopic nuclei (sea salt, sulfates) allow condensation at RH just above 100% (Hygroscopic nuclei).
> 2. **Provide a surface:** CCN act as substrates on which water vapour can condense. Typical sizes: 0.1–1 μm (Aitken nuclei) to 1–10 μm (large nuclei).
> 3. **Determine cloud droplet number:** More CCN → more droplets → smaller average size → brighter cloud (higher albedo). Maritime clouds have fewer but larger droplets; continental clouds have more but smaller droplets (Continental versus maritime contrast).

---

### Question 2: Distinguish between Nimbostratus and Cumulonimbus clouds in terms of formation, structure, and weather.
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Module 1, 3 marks]**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Nimbostratus (Ns) | Cumulonimbus (Cb) |
|---|---|---|
| **Formation** | Gentle, large-scale frontal lifting (warm front) | Strong, localized convective updrafts |
| **Structure** | Horizontal, layered, diffuse, dark grey, base 0.5–2 km, vertically 4–5 km thick | Massive vertical tower; flat dark base, anvil-shaped ice-crystal top reaching tropopause |
| **Composition** | Mixed water + ice, dominated by supercooled droplets | Water at base, mixed middle, ice at anvil |
| **Weather produced** | Continuous, moderate-to-heavy rain/snow lasting hours | Thunderstorms, hail, torrential rain, lightning, gusty winds — short duration (30–60 min) |
| **Areal extent** | Thousands of km² | Localized (1–50 km²) |

> **One-line distinction:** *Nimbostratus = steady "marathon" frontal rain; Cumulonimbus = violent "sprint" convective storm.*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule (Internal Choice):** Module 1 carries one 14-mark question, and students will be offered internal choice between two full 14-mark options. **Both options are provided below.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):**
> **(a) Classify clouds based on altitude and explain the characteristic features of any TWO high-level and TWO low-level cloud genera. (7 marks)**
>
> **(b) A surface air parcel has temperature 28 °C and dew point 18 °C. Estimate the lifting condensation level (LCL) in metres. If the surface pressure is 1005 hPa, find the cloud-base pressure using the hypsometric equation. (7 marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper, Module 1, 14 marks]**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Cloud Classification by Altitude (4 mark content expected):**

Clouds are classified into **4 families** based on the altitude of their **base** (mid-latitude reference):

1. **High Family** — Base > 6,000 m, composed of **ice crystals** only. Genera: Cirrus, Cirrostratus, Cirrocumulus.
2. **Middle Family** — Base 2,000–6,000 m, composed of **water + ice** (mixed phase). Genera: Altostratus, Altocumulus.
3. **Low Family** — Base < 2,000 m, composed of **water droplets** mostly. Genera: Stratus, Stratocumulus, Nimbostratus.
4. **Vertical Development Family** — Base ~500 m, tops reach tropopause. Genera: Cumulus, Cumulonimbus.

**Two High-Level Clouds (1.5 marks each):**

- **Cirrus (Ci):** Detached, delicate, white filamentary "hair-like" clouds. Pure ice crystals. Indicate upper-level moisture and often the **first sign of an approaching warm front** (24–48 hours ahead). No precipitation reaches the ground (virga may occur).
- **Cirrostratus (Cs):** Transparent, whitish veil covering the sky. The sun/moon shines through with a characteristic **22° halo** (formed by refraction through hexagonal ice crystals). Indicates **active warm front** within 12–24 hours, with steady rain expected.
- *(Alternative: Cirrocumulus (Cc) — small white patches in ripples; "mackerel sky"; signals instability at high levels.)*

**Two Low-Level Clouds (1.5 marks each):**

- **Stratus (St):** Grey, uniform, featureless sheet. Often the upper limit of fog (if base touches ground). Associated with **drizzle or stable, gloomy weather**. Forms by gentle radiative cooling or mixing.
- **Nimbostratus (Ns):** Thick, dark grey, diffuse, **continuous rain/snow producer**. The classic warm-front rain cloud. Base 0.5–2 km, vertically extensive (often 4–5 km thick). Critical for sustained hydrological events.
- *(Alternative: Stratocumulus (Sc) — most common cloud on Earth; grey-whitish rolls; weak showers.)*

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1 — Compute LCL using the 125 m/°C rule:**

$$z_{LCL} = 125 \times (T_0 - T_d) = 125 \times (28 - 18) = 125 \times 10 = 1250 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Compute mean temperature of the layer:**

Parcel cools dry-adiabatically at 9.8 °C/km = 0.0098 °C/m.
Temperature at LCL:
$$T_{LCL} = 28 - 0.0098 \times 1250 = 28 - 12.25 = 15.75 \text{ °C} = 288.9 \text{ K}$$

Mean temperature:
$$\bar{T} = \frac{28 + 15.75}{2} = 21.875 \text{ °C} = 295.025 \text{ K}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Hypsometric Equation:**

$$z = \frac{R_d \cdot \bar{T}}{g} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{P_0}{P_{CB}}\right)$$

$$1250 = \frac{287 \times 295.025}{9.81} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{1005}{P_{CB}}\right)$$

$$1250 = 8631.0 \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{1005}{P_{CB}}\right)$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{1005}{P_{CB}}\right) = \frac{1250}{8631.0} = 0.1448$$

$$\frac{1005}{P_{CB}} = e^{0.1448} = 1.1558$$

$$P_{CB} = \frac{1005}{1.1558} = 869.5 \text{ hPa}$$

**Final Answer:**

> $\boxed{z_{LCL} = 1250 \text{ m}, \quad P_{CB} \approx 869.5 \text{ hPa}}$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks):**
> **(a) With neat schematic description, explain the formation, structure, and stages of a Cumulonimbus cloud. Discuss its significance in applied hydrology. (7 marks)**
>
> **(b) Define Lifting Condensation Level (LCL). Derive the empirical relation $z_{LCL} = 125(T_0 - T_d)$. (7 marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper, Module 1, 14 marks]**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Formation (2 marks):**
Cumulonimbus forms when a **warm, moist, unstable air parcel** is lifted vigorously (by surface heating, orographic forcing, or frontal convergence). The parcel rises dry-adiabatically to the LCL, then moist-adiabatically above, with strong **updrafts** (>10 m/s in mature stage) feeding the cloud. Latent heat release sustains buoyancy. The cloud grows vertically through the entire troposphere until it hits the **tropopause**, where the anvil forms.

**Structure (2 marks):**
- **Base (~500 m):** Water droplets, dark and flat.
- **Middle (2–8 km):** Mixed phase (supercooled water + ice); often the **hail formation zone** (-10 °C to -30 °C level).
- **Top (above 9–12 km):** Pure **ice crystals** spreading horizontally to form the **anvil (incus)** at the tropopause.
- **Sub-features:** Mammatus (pouch-like underside), pileus (cap cloud on top), wall cloud (mesocyclone base).

**Stages (2 marks):**
1. **Cumulus Stage:** Only updrafts; cloud consists entirely of water droplets; no precipitation yet.
2. **Mature Stage:** Updrafts and downdrafts coexist; heaviest precipitation; lightning, hail, and gusty winds occur.
3. **Dissipating Stage:** Downdrafts dominate; precipitation weakens; only the anvil (ice crystals) remains.

**Significance in Applied Hydrology (1 mark):**
Cumulonimbus is the **primary cloud responsible for flash floods, hailstorm damage, and urban flooding** in tropical regions like Kerala. Its **high-intensity, short-duration rainfall** (50–200 mm/hr) overwhelms stormwater drainage systems, triggers **landslides in Western Ghats terrain**, and produces the bulk of **monsoon cloudburst events**. Hydrologists must account for Cb storms in **Probable Maximum Precipitation (PMP)** estimates and **spillway design** of dams.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Definition (2 marks):**
The **Lifting Condensation Level (LCL)** is the altitude at which a rising unsaturated air parcel, cooling at the **Dry Adiabatic Lapse Rate (DALR = 9.8 °C/km)**, becomes saturated (Relative Humidity = 100%) and cloud formation begins.

**Derivation of $z_{LCL} = 125 (T_0 - T_d)$ (5 marks):**

**Step 1 — Parcel temperature at height z (below LCL):**

The parcel cools dry-adiabatically:
$$T(z) = T_0 - \Gamma_d \cdot z \quad \text{...(1)}$$
where $\Gamma_d = 0.0098$ °C/m.

**Step 2 — Dew point at height z:**

Empirically, the dew point decreases with height at ~2 °C/km = 0.002 °C/m:
$$T_d(z) = T_d - 0.002 \cdot z \quad \text{...(2)}$$

**Step 3 — Apply saturation condition at LCL:**

At $z = z_{LCL}$, $T(z_{LCL}) = T_d(z_{LCL})$:
$$T_0 - 0.0098 \cdot z_{LCL} = T_d - 0.002 \cdot z_{LCL}$$

**Step 4 — Solve:**
$$T_0 - T_d = (0.0098 - 0.002) \cdot z_{LCL} = 0.0078 \cdot z_{LCL}$$
$$z_{LCL} = \frac{T_0 - T_d}{0.0078} = 128.2 \cdot (T_0 - T_d) \approx 125 \cdot (T_0 - T_d)$$

**Final Answer:**
$$\boxed{z_{LCL} \approx 125 \cdot (T_0 - T_d) \text{ metres}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing cloud base and cloud top:** Cloud family is defined by the **base altitude**, not the top. Cumulus tops can reach 6 km, but its base is at ~500 m. Writing "cumulus is a middle-level cloud" is a **direct 1-mark penalty**.
> 2. **Forgetting units in 125 m/°C rule:** The constant 125 has units of **metres per °C** — students must write the units in the equation or the answer is considered numerically incomplete.
> 3. **Using the wrong lapse rate above LCL:** Below LCL, use **DALR = 9.8 °C/km**; above LCL, use **SALR (≈5–6 °C/km)**. Mixing them up gives wrong cloud thickness and wrong precipitation predictions.
> 4. **Confusing 22° halo with rainbow:** The 22° halo is produced by **ice crystal refraction** (cirrostratus), not raindrops. A rainbow is produced by **water droplet refraction** (rain-bearing clouds). Examiners award ½ mark for this distinction.
> 5. **Not mentioning condensation nuclei:** Any cloud-formation answer without the word "condensation nuclei" or "CCN" is considered **incomplete** for full marks.
> 6. **Misidentifying cumulonimbus as low-cloud:** Cumulonimbus is in the **Vertical Development family** (D), not the Low family (C), despite its low base. This is a 1-mark trap.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION BULLETS — KTU 2024 Scheme Style**

- [x] **Cloud** = visible aggregate of water droplets and/or ice crystals suspended in air.
- [x] **10 Principal Genera** (WMO International Cloud Atlas).
- [x] **4 Families** = High (ice), Middle (mixed), Low (water), Vertical (water-to-ice).
- [x] **Latin roots:** Cirrus = hair, Cumulus = heap, Stratus = layer, Nimbus = rain, Alto = middle.
- [x] **High clouds (Ci, Cs, Cc):** Above 6,000 m, all ice crystals.
- [x] **Middle clouds (As, Ac):** 2,000–6,000 m, mixed water/ice.
- [x] **Low clouds (St, Sc, Ns):** Below 2,000 m, water droplets; **Ns produces continuous rain**.
- [x] **Vertical clouds (Cu, Cb):** Base ~500 m, extend vertically to tropopause; **Cb produces thunderstorms**.
- [x] **22° halo** = diagnostic of **Cirrostratus** (ice-crystal refraction).
- [x] **"Mackerel sky"** = **Cirrocumulus** (high ripples).
- [x] **"Watery sun"** = **Altostratus** (sun seen through translucent sheet).
- [x] **Anvil top (Incus)** = diagnostic of mature **Cumulonimbus** at tropopause.
- [x] **DALR** = 9.8 °C/km (below LCL); **SALR** ≈ 5–6 °C/km (above LCL).
- [x] **LCL formula** = $z_{LCL} = 125(T_0 - T_d)$ metres.
- [x] **Hypsometric equation** = $z = \frac{R_d \bar{T}}{g} \ln(P_0/P)$.
- [x] **Tetens equation** = $e_s(T) = 6.112 \cdot \exp(17.67 T / (T + 243.5))$ in hPa, T in °C.
- [x] **Condensation nuclei (CCN)** are **essential** for cloud formation; without them, RH > 400% would be required.
- [x] **Nimbostratus** = steady frontal rain (12–24 h); **Cumulonimbus** = violent convective storm (30–60 min).
- [x] **Cumulonimbus stages:** Cumulus → Mature → Dissipating.
- [x] **Hail forms** in Cumulonimbus between the -10 °C and -30 °C isotherms (in the mixed-phase zone).
- [x] **Cloud families are based on base altitude**, NOT top altitude.
- [x] **Most common cloud on Earth** = **Stratocumulus**.
- [x] **Tallest clouds** = **Cumulonimbus** (tops reach 12–18 km in tropics).
- [x] **Hydrological importance:** Cumulonimbus = flash flood driver; Nimbostratus = riverine flood driver.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Monsoon - Characteristics of Indian Summer Monsoon Rainfall, Climate Oscillations and Indian Monsoon Rainfall, Evapotranspiration - Methods of Estimation - Blaney Criddle Method (Problem), Penman Method, Penman-Monteith Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET526 - APPLIED HYDROLOGY AND CLIMATOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Monsoon - Characteristics of Indian Summer Monsoon Rainfall, Climate Oscillations and Indian Monsoon Rainfall, Evapotranspiration - Methods of Estimation - Blaney Criddle Method (Problem), Penman Method, Penman-Monteith Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Monsoon — Formal Academic Definition

A **monsoon** is a thermally driven planetary-scale atmospheric circulation system, characterized by a complete seasonal reversal of prevailing wind direction over a region, accompanied by distinct wet (rainy) and dry seasons. It arises from the differential heating between continental landmasses and adjacent oceans, producing a low-pressure trough over the heated land in summer and a reversal in winter.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (PED — Water Resources):** The Indian Summer Monsoon (ISM) is a coupled ocean–atmosphere phenomenon in which low-level southwesterly winds (the **Somali / Findlater Jet**) transport moisture from the Indian Ocean onto the Indian subcontinent between **June and September**, contributing approximately **75% of India's annual rainfall**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — A "Breathing" Continent

Imagine the Indian subcontinent as a giant warm brick placed next to the cool Indian Ocean. In summer, the brick heats up, the air above it rises (low pressure), and cooler, moist ocean air rushes in to "breathe in" — this is the **Southwest Monsoon**. In winter, the brick cools faster than the ocean, the pressure reverses, and dry continental air flows outward toward the ocean — this is the **Northeast (Winter) Monsoon**. The continent is literally inhaling and exhaling moisture across the seasons.

## 1.3 Characteristics of Indian Summer Monsoon Rainfall (ISMR)

The ISMR exhibits several distinguishing features crucial for hydrological design:

- **Onset (arrival):** Around **1 June over Kerala (Kokatatmy criterion based on OLR & rainfall)**.
- **Withdrawal:** Begins in **September from the northwest**, fully retreats by mid-October.
- **Duration:** ~110–120 days (June–September, JJAS).
- **Annual Contribution:** ~75% of India's total annual rainfall; ~90% along the west coast.
- **Active & Break Spells:** Continuous wet periods (3–4 days) alternate with dry breaks (5–7 days) within the monsoon season.
- **Spatial Variability:** Extremely high — Cherrapunji (~11,000 mm/yr) vs. Rajasthan (~100 mm/yr).
- **Temporal Variability:** Coefficient of variation ~10% (all-India), but **>30% at station scale**.
- **Inter-annual Variability:** Governed by large-scale climate oscillations (ENSO, IOD, NAO, MJO).
- **Progress Mechanism:** Propagates through **Mascarene High → Cross-Equatorial Flow → Somali Jet → Arabian Sea Branch + Bay of Bengal Branch**, converging over central India.
- **Standardized ISMR Index:** Rainfall between June and September averaged over the Indian landmass, normalized by its long-term mean and standard deviation (used by IMD).

> [!NOTE]
> The **Southwest Monsoon Current (SMC)** advects moisture across the equator at a depth of ~3 km, carrying **5–6 × 10⁶ m³/s of moisture flux**, making it one of the strongest moisture currents on Earth.

## 1.4 Climate Oscillations Affecting Indian Monsoon

| Oscillation | Acronym | Domain | Periodicity | Indian Monsoon Linkage |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| El Niño-Southern Oscillation | **ENSO** | Tropical Pacific | 2–7 years | El Niño → weaker ISM; La Niña → stronger ISM |
| Indian Ocean Dipole | **IOD** | Tropical Indian Ocean | 30–90 days (seasonal) | Positive IOD → stronger ISM (offsets El Niño) |
| Madden-Julian Oscillation | **MJO** | Tropical belt (30°E–180°) | 30–60 days | Active MJO phases modulate sub-seasonal spells |
| North Atlantic Oscillation | **NAO** | North Atlantic | Multi-day to decades | NAO+ → enhanced Western Disturbances |
| Atlantic Multidecadal Oscillation | **AMO** | North Atlantic | 60–80 years | AMO+ correlates with multi-decadal monsoon weakening |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dipole Mode Index (DMI):** DMI = SST anomaly (western pole 50°E–70°E, 10°S–10°N) − SST anomaly (southeastern pole 90°E–110°E, 10°S–0°). Positive DMI (>0.4 °C) historically offsets ~50% of El Niño's suppressive effect on ISM.

## 1.5 Evapotranspiration (ET) — Definition

**Evapotranspiration (ET)** is the combined process by which water is transferred from the soil and plant surfaces to the atmosphere through:
1. **Evaporation (E)** — physical conversion of liquid water to vapor from soil, water bodies, and wet surfaces.
2. **Transpiration (T)** — biological release of water vapor through plant stomata.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reference Evapotranspiration (ET_o or ETo):** The ET rate from a hypothetical reference crop (short grass, 0.12 m height, surface resistance 70 s/m, albedo 0.23) that is actively growing, well-watered, and completely shading the ground. This is the standardized benchmark for all methods discussed.

## 1.6 Conceptual Analogy — A Two-Layer Evaporating Sponge

Think of a wet sponge covered partially by a plant canopy as **two evaporating layers**:
- The **canopy (top layer)** transpires through leaf stomata — this is a regulated process governed by solar radiation and vapor pressure deficit.
- The **soil (bottom layer)** evaporates when wet and exposed — governed by soil moisture and radiation at the surface.
- The **atmosphere above** demands water vapor based on its dryness and wind speed.
- **Net ET = (Energy available) − (Aerodynamic resistance) − (Surface resistance)**.

This is precisely the **Penman-Monteith** framework — an "energy–supply–demand balance."

## 1.7 Methods of Estimating ET (Module Focus)

| Method | Type | Inputs Required | Era | Accuracy |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Blaney-Criddle** | Temperature-based (Empirical) | Temp, latitude, crop factor | 1950 | Low–Moderate |
| **Penman** | Combination (Energy + Aero) | Temp, RH, wind, radiation | 1948 | High |
| **Penman-Monteith (FAO-56)** | Combination (Physically based) | Temp, RH, wind, radiation, surface resistance | 1990+ | Very High (Standard) |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Climate Oscillations Matter for Monsoon Forecasting

The ISM is a **chaotic coupled system** with both predictable and stochastic components. Climate oscillations introduce **boundary forcing** (e.g., SST anomalies in remote oceans) that modulates the monsoon trough, tropical convergence zones, and the strength of the Somali Jet. **Statistical + dynamical models** that ingest ENSO and IOD indices outperform persistence forecasts by 15–25% in skill score (RMSE-based).

> [!NOTE]
> **Walker Circulation:** A zonal east–west overturning cell along the equatorial Pacific. During El Niño, the ascending branch shifts eastward, suppressing the Walker cell over the Indian Ocean, weakening monsoon convection. The Southern Oscillation Index (SOI) is the surface signature: SOI = Tahiti MSLP − Darwin MSLP (negative SOI → El Niño conditions).

## 2.2 ISMR Variability and Standardized Indices

### KTU Formula Sheet — Monsoon & Climate Indices

| Symbol | Quantity | Formula / Definition | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| ISMR | All-India Summer Monsoon Rainfall | Weighted mean of JJAS rainfall over 36 met subdivisions | mm |
| $\sigma_{\text{ISMR}}$ | Standard deviation of ISMR | $\sqrt{\sum (R_i - \bar{R})^2 / (n-1)}$ | mm |
| CV | Coefficient of variation | $\sigma_{\text{ISMR}} / \bar{R} \times 100$ | % |
| SOI | Southern Oscillation Index | $\text{MSLP}_{\text{Tahiti}} - \text{MSLP}_{\text{Darwin}}$ | hPa (standardized) |
| DMI | Dipole Mode Index | $\Delta \text{SST}_{\text{W pole}} - \Delta \text{SST}_{\text{E pole}}$ | °C |
| OLR | Outgoing Longwave Radiation | Satellite-measured top-of-atmosphere IR flux | W/m² |
| TBO | Tropospheric Biennial Oscillation | ENSO + IOD phase-locked alternation | years |

> [!TIP]
> For **2-mark / 3-mark KTU short questions**, memorize: *ISMR contributes ~75% of India's annual rainfall; ENSO has a correlation of −0.4 to −0.6 with ISMR; IOD has +0.3 to +0.5 correlation with ISMR.*

---

## 2.3 Blaney-Criddle Method — Theoretical Foundation

### 2.3.1 The Concept

Proposed by **Howard Blaney & Wayne Criddle (1950, USDA-SCS)**, this is a **temperature-driven empirical method** that estimates consumptive use (CU) as a function of mean monthly temperature and the **percentage of annual daytime hours** at a given latitude.

The **central hypothesis**: ET is proportional to the *consumptive use factor* (f), which in turn depends on the **heat available** (mean temperature) and the **daylight duration** (p).

### 2.3.2 Step-by-Step Logic

1. Compute **mean monthly temperature** $T$ (°C) for the growing month.
2. Look up **p** (mean daily % of annual daytime hours for the month, latitude-dependent).
3. Compute the monthly consumptive use factor: $u = p \cdot T / 100$.
4. Apply a **crop coefficient (K_c)** to obtain actual crop ET: $ET_c = K_c \cdot u$.

### 2.3.3 Why It Works (and Fails)

- **Strengths:** Requires only temperature data (widely available), simple spreadsheet math.
- **Weaknesses:** Ignores humidity, wind, and net radiation; **underestimates in humid tropics, overestimates in arid regions**.

> [!NOTE]
> The **Blaney-Criddle formula was superseded by the Penman-Monteith (FAO-56)** in 1998 as the global standard reference. However, it remains a KTU syllabus topic for its historical and conceptual clarity.

---

## 2.4 Penman Method — Theoretical Foundation

### 2.4.1 The Concept

**Howard Penman (1948, Rothamsted, UK)** derived the first physically-based combination equation combining:
- **Energy balance** (radiation-driven ET, weighted by $\Delta$).
- **Aerodynamic** (vapor pressure deficit + wind-driven ET, weighted by $\gamma$).

The combination avoids the need to choose *either* energy *or* aerodynamic forcing — it **weights them** by the relative slopes of the saturation vapor pressure curve and the psychrometric constant.

### 2.4.2 Operational Logic

1. Compute net radiation $R_n$ (MJ/m²/day) using solar radiation, albedo, and temperature.
2. Compute soil heat flux $G$ (often ~0 for daily; small for monthly).
3. Compute the slope of the saturation vapor pressure curve $\Delta$ at mean air temperature.
4. Compute psychrometric constant $\gamma$.
5. Compute vapor pressure deficit $(e_s - e_a)$ from temperature and humidity data.
6. Apply wind function $f(u_2)$.
7. Combine via the Penman equation.

### 2.4.3 Why It Works (and Fails)

- **Strengths:** Physically rigorous; works well in temperate climates.
- **Weaknesses:** Empirically calibrated for **open water** (Penman originally derived for $E_p$); for crop surfaces requires empirical crop coefficients; aerodynamic term often uses an open-pan-like wind function.

---

## 2.5 Penman-Monteith Method — Theoretical Foundation (FAO-56)

### 2.5.1 The Concept

**Monteith (1965)** refined Penman's energy balance by explicitly including:
- **Aerodynamic resistance** $r_a$ — resistance to vapor transport above the canopy.
- **Surface (canopy/stomatal) resistance** $r_s$ — resistance to vapor transfer through stomata and soil.

**FAO-56 (Allen et al., 1998)** standardized a **reference crop definition** with $r_s = 70$ s/m, $r_a$ parameterized by wind speed, producing the global benchmark equation.

### 2.5.2 Operational Logic

1. Compute $R_n$, $G$, $\Delta$, $\gamma$ as in Penman.
2. Compute mean wind speed $u_2$ at 2 m height (convert from 10 m if needed).
3. Compute $e_s$, $e_a$, and VPD $= e_s - e_a$.
4. Compute $r_a = 208/u_2$ (s/m, FAO-56).
5. Use the standardized $r_s = 70$ s/m for the reference crop.
6. Apply the **FAO-56 Penman-Monteith equation**.

### 2.5.3 Why It Is the Global Standard

- **Strengths:** Eliminates empirical crop coefficient calibration for the *reference* surface; physically based; works across climates.
- **Weaknesses:** Requires full meteorological data set (T, RH, u, R_s or sunshine hours); higher data demand.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **K TU High-Yield Takeaway:** The FAO-56 Penman-Monteith equation is the **only method recognized by the UN FAO, WMO, and ICID** as the international standard for $ET_o$ estimation. Always cite it for irrigation water requirement problems.

---

## 2.6 Comprehensive KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Method | Governing Equation | Key Parameters | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Blaney-Criddle | $ET_o = p \cdot T / 100$ (mm/day, after units) | $p$, $T$, $K_c$ | T in °C, p from latitude table |
| Modified Blaney-Criddle (FAO-24) | $ET_c = a + b \cdot [p \cdot T / (T + 15)]$ | $a$, $b$ (correction) | Includes humidity correction |
| Penman (Open Water) | $E_p = \dfrac{\Delta}{\Delta + \gamma} \cdot (R_n - G)/\lambda + \dfrac{\gamma}{\Delta + \gamma} \cdot f(u_2) \cdot (e_s - e_a)$ | $R_n$, $G$, $f(u_2)$ | $\lambda$ = latent heat (2.45 MJ/kg) |
| Penman Wind Function | $f(u_2) = 2.6 \cdot (1 + 0.54 \cdot u_2)$ | $u_2$ in m/s | Returns E in mm/day |
| Psychrometric Constant | $\gamma = c_p \cdot P / (0.622 \cdot \lambda)$ | $P$ in kPa | $\gamma \approx 0.066$ kPa/°C at sea level |
| Saturation Vapor Pressure | $e_s(T) = 0.6108 \cdot \exp[17.27 T / (T + 237.3)]$ | $T$ in °C | Returns kPa |
| Slope of Saturation Curve | $\Delta = 4098 \cdot e_s(T) / (T + 237.3)^2$ | $T$ in °C | kPa/°C |
| FAO-56 Penman-Monteith | $ET_o = \dfrac{0.408 \cdot \Delta \cdot (R_n - G) + \gamma \cdot \dfrac{900}{T + 273} \cdot u_2 \cdot (e_s - e_a)}{\Delta + \gamma \cdot (1 + 0.34 \cdot u_2)}$ | All-met data | $T$ in °C, $u_2$ in m/s, $R_n, G$ in MJ/m²/day, output in mm/day |

> [!TIP]
> Memorize the **FAO-56 PM** equation verbatim — it appears in nearly every KTU module question and is worth ~14 marks per Part B question.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Blaney-Criddle Method — Worked Problem

### Problem Statement (KTU-Style, 7–10 marks)

> A region is located at latitude 20°N. The mean monthly temperature for July is 28.5 °C. The mean percentage of annual daytime hours (p) for July at 20°N is **9.3** (i.e., 9.3% of annual daytime hours occur in July at 20°N). Using the **Blaney-Criddle method**, compute the monthly consumptive use (reference ET) for July in mm, given that the unit-conversion constant (B) is 5.0 mm/°C (or use the direct $p \cdot T / 100$ form). Assume a crop coefficient $K_c = 1.05$ for a well-watered maize crop.

### Solution

**Step 1: Identify the standard Blaney-Criddle consumptive use equation.**

For monthly estimation, the Blaney-Criddle formula is expressed in two equivalent ways:

$$
u = \frac{p \cdot T}{100} \quad \text{(form A: yields dimensionless "use factor")}
$$

or in metric, the monthly ET in mm is approximated as:

$$
ET_o \; (\text{mm/month}) = p \cdot T \cdot c
$$

where $c$ is a **units-conversion / crop factor** (often $c = 0.457$ for °C-inches and $c \approx 1.0$ for °C-mm at $T \approx 25$ °C — for direct $K_c$-style use, we adopt the simpler $u$ definition multiplied by the appropriate $K_c$).

**For this problem**, we use the **direct consumptive use definition** that yields depth when the **p × T** product is multiplied by the **mean-daylight fraction** (already in %) and a **monthly heat-unit scaling**. The classical USDA form gives:

$$
u \; (\text{mm/month}) = \frac{p \cdot T \cdot K_c \cdot n}{100}
$$

where $n$ = number of days in the month. For simplicity in KTU-2024 notation, the **monthly consumptive use** is:

$$
u = \frac{p \cdot T}{100} \quad \text{(in cm when T is in °C, p is daily %)}
$$

Multiply by 10 to get mm:

$$
u \; (\text{mm}) = \frac{p \cdot T \cdot 10 \cdot K_c \cdot n}{100}
$$

Wait — let us use the **most widely adopted KTU form** to avoid confusion:

**Step 2: Apply the standard monthly Blaney-Criddle consumptive use formula.**

$$
u = \frac{p \cdot T}{40} \quad \text{(monthly, in inches for T in °F)}
$$

In the **metric/°C version** (popular in KTU textbooks and Subramanya's *Engineering Hydrology*):

$$
u_{\text{monthly}} = p \cdot T \cdot K_t \quad \text{(mm)}
$$

where $K_t$ is a temperature coefficient ≈ 0.45 to 0.50 for tropical stations. For the present problem, assume $K_t = 0.46$ (typical for warm-temperate to tropical latitudes).

> [!NOTE]
> Multiple equivalent formulations of the Blaney-Criddle exist in textbooks. The most defensible KTU form is:
> $$ET_o \, (\text{mm/month}) = \frac{p \cdot \bar{T} \cdot K_c \cdot 0.46 \cdot 30.4}{100}$$
> where 30.4 = average days per month, 0.46 = latent heat–temperature coefficient, and p is mean daily % of annual daytime hours.

**Step 3: Plug in the numbers.**

- $p = 9.3$
- $\bar{T} = 28.5$ °C
- $K_c = 1.05$
- $K_t = 0.46$
- $n = 31$ (July)

$$
ET_o = \frac{9.3 \times 28.5 \times 1.05 \times 0.46 \times 31}{100}
$$

Compute the numerator step by step:

$$
9.3 \times 28.5 = 265.05
$$

$$
265.05 \times 1.05 = 278.30
$$

$$
278.30 \times 0.46 = 128.02
$$

$$
128.02 \times 31 = 3968.55
$$

$$
ET_o = \frac{3968.55}{100} = 39.69 \; \text{mm/month}
$$

**Step 4: Convert to daily ET (if required).**

$$
ET_o^{\text{daily}} = \frac{39.69}{31} = 1.28 \; \text{mm/day}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Final answer for the Blaney-Criddle problem:**
> - $ET_o$ (July) ≈ **39.7 mm/month** for the reference crop.
> - $ET_c$ (Maize, $K_c = 1.05$) ≈ **41.7 mm/month**.
> - Daily equivalent ≈ **1.28 mm/day**.

### 3.1.1 Alternative Simplified Form (Direct mm Output)

A common KTU textbook shortcut (Subramanya, $ET = p \cdot T / 100$, multiplied by 10 and $K_c$):

$$
ET_o \, (\text{mm}) = \frac{p \cdot T}{10} = \frac{9.3 \times 28.5}{10} = 26.5 \; \text{mm}
$$

This is the **mean daily consumptive use** (i.e., the $p$ in the formula is interpreted as the monthly % already scaled). Different textbooks give different unit-conversion factors — **always read the problem statement carefully** and state your assumption.

---

## 3.2 Penman Method — Derivation of the Combination Equation

### 3.2.1 The Physical Setup

Penman starts with two independent equations:

**(a) Energy balance** — ET energy $\lambda ET$ equals the available energy $(R_n - G)$ plus/minus the Bowen ratio-weighted sensible heat:

$$
\lambda ET = \frac{\Delta (R_n - G)}{\Delta + \gamma}
$$

**(b) Aerodynamic** — ET driven by vapor pressure deficit and wind:

$$
\lambda ET = \frac{\gamma \cdot f(u_2) \cdot (e_s - e_a)}{\Delta + \gamma}
$$

**Step 1: Add both contributions to get the total ET.**

Since the energy-balance and aerodynamic components occur in parallel (both must be satisfied), the total is the sum:

$$
\lambda ET = \frac{\Delta (R_n - G) + \gamma \cdot f(u_2) \cdot (e_s - e_a)}{\Delta + \gamma}
$$

**Step 2: Convert energy to depth of water by dividing by $\lambda$ (latent heat of vaporization, 2.45 MJ/kg).**

$$
E = \frac{1}{\lambda} \cdot \frac{\Delta (R_n - G) + \gamma \cdot f(u_2) \cdot (e_s - e_a)}{\Delta + \gamma}
$$

**Step 3: Rearrange to the familiar Penman form.**

$$
E = \frac{\Delta}{\Delta + \gamma} \cdot \frac{R_n - G}{\lambda} + \frac{\gamma}{\Delta + \gamma} \cdot f(u_2) \cdot (e_s - e_a)
$$

**Step 4: Show how the wind function enters.**

The classical Penman wind function for **open water evaporation** (with $u_2$ in m/s, $E$ in mm/day):

$$
f(u_2) = 2.6 \cdot (1 + 0.54 \cdot u_2)
$$

### 3.2.2 Interpretation of the Two Weights

- The **first term** (energy-driven) has weight $\Delta / (\Delta + \gamma)$. At high temperatures $\Delta$ is large → energy dominates (tropical, hot-arid).
- The **second term** (advection-driven) has weight $\gamma / (\Delta + \gamma)$. In humid, low-wind conditions → advection term is small (temperate).
- The **ratio $\Delta / \gamma$** is the "psychrometric dominance." At 20 °C, $\Delta \approx 1.45$, $\gamma \approx 0.066$, so the energy term is weighted ~95% — energy-driven regimes.

---

## 3.3 Penman-Monteith Method — Derivation of FAO-56 Standard Form

### 3.3.1 Starting Point: Energy Balance + Resistance Analogy

The full Penman-Monteith equation (per unit area, per unit time):

$$
\lambda ET = \frac{\Delta (R_n - G) + \rho_a c_p (e_s - e_a) / r_a}{\Delta + \gamma \left(1 + \dfrac{r_s}{r_a}\right)}
$$

**Derivation Step 1: Sensible heat flux (H) from resistance form.**

Sensible heat flux is:

$$
H = \frac{\rho_a c_p (T_s - T_a)}{r_{ah}}
$$

**Step 2: Latent heat flux (λET) from canopy–air vapor gradient.**

$$
\lambda ET = \frac{\rho_a c_p (e_s^{\text{surf}} - e_a)}{\gamma (r_{ah} + r_s)}
$$

**Step 3: Combine using the Bowen ratio $\beta = H / \lambda ET$ and eliminate $T_s$ and $e_s^{\text{surf}}$.**

For a saturated surface, the Clausius-Clapeyron slope gives:

$$
\Delta = \frac{e_s^{\text{surf}} - e_s^{\text{air}}}{T_s - T_a}
$$

Solving simultaneously for $\lambda ET$ yields the **Penman-Monteith combination equation**:

$$
\lambda ET = \frac{\Delta (R_n - G) + \rho_a c_p (e_s - e_a) / r_a}{\Delta + \gamma (1 + r_s / r_a)}
$$

### 3.3.2 FAO-56 Substitution

For the **FAO-56 reference crop** (short grass):
- $r_s = 70$ s/m (bulk surface resistance)
- $r_a = 208 / u_2$ s/m (aerodynamic resistance, $u_2$ = wind speed at 2 m in m/s)
- $\rho_a c_p \approx 0.00101 \cdot (1013 / P) \cdot 1.013 \cdot 10^{-3} \cdot T_a \cdot c_p$ ≈ reduced to a constant $900$ K·mm·kPa·(kg/m³)⁻¹·... simplified.

The FAO-56 simplified form (yielding $ET_o$ in mm/day) is:

$$
ET_o = \frac{0.408 \cdot \Delta \cdot (R_n - G) + \gamma \cdot \dfrac{900}{T_{\text{mean}} + 273} \cdot u_2 \cdot (e_s - e_a)}{\Delta + \gamma \cdot (1 + 0.34 \cdot u_2)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The constant **900** comes from: $\rho_a c_p / (0.622 \cdot \lambda \cdot 0.408) \cdot 208 \approx 900$ under standard conditions. The **0.34** term arises from $r_s / r_a = 70 \cdot u_2 / 208 = 0.337 \cdot u_2 \approx 0.34 \cdot u_2$.

### 3.3.3 Worked Numerical Anchor (FAO-56)

**Given:** $T_{\text{mean}} = 25$ °C, $u_2 = 2.0$ m/s, $R_n = 14$ MJ/m²/day, $G = 0$, $RH_{\text{mean}} = 60\%$, $P = 101.3$ kPa.

**Step 1: $\gamma$ (psychrometric constant).**

$$
\gamma = \frac{c_p \cdot P}{0.622 \cdot \lambda} = \frac{1.013 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 101.3}{0.622 \cdot 2.45} \approx 0.0677 \; \text{kPa/°C}
$$

**Step 2: Saturation vapor pressure at 25 °C.**

$$
e_s(25) = 0.6108 \cdot \exp\left(\frac{17.27 \times 25}{25 + 237.3}\right) = 0.6108 \cdot \exp(1.649) = 0.6108 \times 5.20 = 3.18 \; \text{kPa}
$$

**Step 3: Actual vapor pressure.**

$$
e_a = e_s \cdot \frac{RH}{100} = 3.18 \times 0.60 = 1.91 \; \text{kPa}
$$

**Step 4: VPD.**

$$
e_s - e_a = 3.18 - 1.91 = 1.27 \; \text{kPa}
$$

**Step 5: Slope of saturation curve $\Delta$ at 25 °C.**

$$
\Delta = \frac{4098 \times e_s(T)}{(T + 237.3)^2} = \frac{4098 \times 3.18}{(262.3)^2} = \frac{13037.6}{68800.3} = 0.1895 \; \text{kPa/°C}
$$

**Step 6: Numerator.**

$$
\text{Num} = 0.408 \times 0.1895 \times 14 + 0.0677 \times \frac{900}{298} \times 2.0 \times 1.27
$$

$$
= 1.082 + 0.0677 \times 3.020 \times 2.0 \times 1.27
$$

$$
= 1.082 + 0.519 = 1.601 \; \text{mm/day}
$$

**Step 7: Denominator.**

$$
\text{Den} = 0.1895 + 0.0677 \times (1 + 0.34 \times 2.0) = 0.1895 + 0.0677 \times 1.68 = 0.1895 + 0.1137 = 0.3032
$$

**Step 8: Final $ET_o$.**

$$
ET_o = \frac{1.601}{0.3032} = 5.28 \; \text{mm/day}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Sanity check:** 5.28 mm/day in a humid tropical station is realistic (typical range 3–7 mm/day in coastal India during pre-monsoon).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — ET Methods

| Feature | Blaney-Criddle | Penman | Penman-Monteith (FAO-56) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Data required | $T$, latitude | $T$, $RH$, $u$, $R_n$ | $T$, $RH$, $u$, $R_n$ (or $n/N$ or $R_s$) |
| Climate suitability | Humid-temperate to tropical | Temperate, semi-arid | All climates |
| Physical basis | Empirical | Semi-empirical (energy + aero) | Fully physical (resistance) |
| Surface resistance | Implicit in $K_c$ | Ignored | Explicit ($r_s = 70$ s/m) |
| Skill vs. lysimeter | RMSE ~1.5–2.0 mm/day | RMSE ~0.7–1.2 mm/day | RMSE ~0.3–0.6 mm/day |
| KTU weightage (typical) | 3–4 marks | 4–5 marks | 6–8 marks |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Climate Oscillations & Indian Monsoon Linkages

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Climate Oscillations] --> B[ENSO]
    A --> C[IOD]
    A --> D[NAO]
    A --> E[MJO]
    A --> F[AMO]

    B --> B1[El Nino Positive SST]
    B --> B2[La Nina Negative SST]
    B1 --> G[Suppresses Walker Cell]
    B2 --> H[Strengthens Walker Cell]
    G --> I[Weakened ISMR]
    H --> J[Enhanced ISMR]

    C --> C1[Positive IOD DMI Greater 0.4]
    C --> C2[Negative IOD DMI Less Minus 0.4]
    C1 --> J2[Strengthens ISMR]
    C2 --> I2[Weakens ISMR]

    E --> E1[Active Phase over Indian Ocean]
    E1 --> K[Enhanced Convection]
    K --> J

    I --> M[All India Summer Monsoon Rainfall]
    J --> M
    I2 --> M
    J2 --> M

    M --> N[Hydrological Design Input]
    M --> O[Agricultural Planning]
    M --> P[Reservoir Operation]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each oscillation feeds its own teleconnection pathway, but they interact (e.g., a positive IOD can offset a co-occurring El Niño). The convergence point is **ISMR**, which is the input for downstream hydrological applications.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Indian Summer Monsoon Life Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[April Heat Low over Tibet] --> B[April May Cross Equatorial Flow Begins]
    B --> C[June 1 Onset over Kerala]
    C --> D[June July Advance up to NW India]
    D --> E[July August Peak Monsoon]
    E --> F[Active and Break Spells]
    F --> G[September Withdrawal from NW]
    G --> H[October Full Retreat]
    H --> I[Nov Dec NE Monsoon Onset]

    subgraph Inputs
    T1[Arabian Sea Branch]
    T2[Bay of Bengal Branch]
    end

    T1 --> C
    T2 --> C
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Comparison of ET Estimation Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Available Meteorological Data] --> Decision1{Do you have full data set Temp RH Wind Radiation?}
    Decision1 -- No Temperature only --> A[Blaney Criddle Method]
    Decision1 -- Yes Full Data --> Decision2{Is surface resistance known or assumed?}

    Decision2 -- Empirical Kc --> B[Penman Method with Kc]
    Decision2 -- Reference Crop FAO 56 --> C[Penman Monteith FAO 56]

    A --> Out1[ET estimate mm per day]
    B --> Out1
    C --> Out1

    Out1 --> Application[Hydrological Design Crop Water Requirement Reservoir Inflow Estimation]
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Block — Penman-Monteith Equation Anatomy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[FAO 56 Penman Monteith] --> B[Radiation Term]
    A --> C[Aerodynamic Term]
    A --> D[Resistance Coupling]

    B --> B1[0.408 x Delta x Rn minus G]
    C --> C1[gamma x 900 divided by T plus 273 x u2 x es minus ea]
    D --> D1[Delta plus gamma x 1 plus 0.34 x u2]

    B1 --> Out[ET o mm per day]
    C1 --> Out
    D1 --> Out
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation of the two terms in the numerator:**
> - **Radiation term** (B1): Energy-limited evaporation — dominant in calm, radiation-rich conditions.
> - **Aerodynamic term** (C1): Atmosphere-controlled evaporation — dominant in dry, windy (advective) conditions.
> - **Resistance coupling** (D1): The denominator penalizes evaporation when $r_s$ is high (stomata closed at night, drought stress) or when $r_a$ is low (low wind).

---

## 4.5 Mermaid Decision Tree — Choosing the Right ET Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Data Availability} -->|Temperature only| R1[Blaney Criddle]
    Q1 -->|Full met data| Q2{Climate Type}
    Q2 -->|Humid Temperate| R2[Penman]
    Q2 -->|All climates Standard| R3[Penman Monteith FAO 56]
    Q2 -->|Arid Hot| R4[Penman Monteith with adjusted rs]
    R1 --> Application[Compute ET o]
    R2 --> Application
    R3 --> Application
    R4 --> Application
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 1]

**Q:** List any three distinguishing characteristics of the Indian Summer Monsoon Rainfall (ISMR) and briefly explain the role of the El Niño–Southern Oscillation (ENSO) in modulating ISMR.

**Model Answer:**

**Three characteristics of ISMR:**
1. **Seasonal concentration:** ISMR (June–September) contributes ~75% of India's annual rainfall, with the southwest monsoon advancing in pulses from Kerala (~1 June) to NW India by mid-July.
2. **High spatial variability:** Annual rainfall ranges from <100 mm (western Rajasthan) to >11,000 mm (Meghalaya), governed by orography, the Somali Jet trajectory, and monsoon trough positioning.
3. **Active-break spells:** Within the season, continuous 3–4 day wet periods (active) alternate with 5–7 day dry periods (break), modulated by the Madden-Julian Oscillation and mid-tropospheric cyclones.

**Role of ENSO:**
- El Niño events (positive SST anomalies in the central-eastern Pacific) **weaken the Walker circulation over the Indian Ocean**, suppress convection over the Indian subcontinent, and reduce the strength of the Somali Jet, leading to **deficit ISMR** (correlation coefficient ≈ −0.5).
- La Niña events have the opposite effect, producing **excess ISMR**.
- The correlation is **stronger in El Niño years** and is modulated by the IOD — a positive IOD can offset ~50% of El Niño's suppressive effect.

*(Self-contained answer; 3 marks distributed: 1.5 marks for characteristics, 1.5 marks for ENSO role.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1]

**Q:** Define **Reference Evapotranspiration (ET_o)**. Why is the **FAO-56 Penman-Monteith** method considered the global standard for ET_o estimation?

**Model Answer:**

**Definition of ET_o:**
Reference Evapotranspiration (ET_o) is the rate of evapotranspiration from a hypothetical reference crop (short grass, 0.12 m height, albedo 0.23, surface resistance 70 s/m), actively growing, well-watered, and completely shading the ground. It represents the atmospheric evaporative demand, independent of crop type, soil, and water availability.

**Why FAO-56 Penman-Monteith is the global standard:**
1. **Physically based:** It explicitly models both the **energy balance** (radiation-driven) and the **aerodynamic** (wind- and humidity-driven) components, with explicit **surface and aerodynamic resistances**.
2. **Climatically robust:** Validated against lysimeter data across all climate types (humid, arid, semi-arid, temperate).
3. **Standardized parameters:** The reference crop definition ($r_s = 70$ s/m, $h = 0.12$ m, $a = 0.23$) eliminates ambiguity, allowing global data interchange.
4. **Endorsed by WMO, FAO, and ICID** as the international standard for irrigation scheduling and water resource planning.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for justification.)*

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 1, Compulsory Set]

**Q:** *(a)* Describe the **characteristics of the Indian Summer Monsoon Rainfall (ISMR)** with reference to its onset, advance, withdrawal, spatial distribution, and inter-annual variability. Discuss the role of **ENSO and IOD** in modulating ISMR. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* The mean monthly temperature for a station at **latitude 22°N** during the month of August is **29.0 °C**. The mean daily percentage of annual daytime hours for August at 22°N is **9.1%**. Compute the **monthly reference evapotranspiration** (in mm) using the **Blaney-Criddle method**, given a crop coefficient $K_c = 1.10$ for a well-watered paddy crop. Use the simplified metric form $ET_o \, (\text{mm}) = p \cdot T / 10$ for the daily equivalent, and multiply by 31 (days in August) to get the monthly value. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**1. Onset & Advance:**
- **Onset date (arrival over Kerala):** ~1 June, defined by the **Kokatatmy criterion** (OLR ≤ 200 W/m², persistent rainfall, zonal wind shift).
- **Advance:** Propagates in 3–4 pulses through the **Arabian Sea Branch** (reaching Mumbai ~10 June, Delhi ~1 July) and the **Bay of Bengal Branch** (reaching NE India, then curving NW).
- The **monsoon trough** at the surface extends from the heat low over Pakistan across the Ganges plains to the Bay of Bengal.

**2. Withdrawal:**
- Begins in **early September from NW India** (Rajasthan first).
- Retreats SE; full withdrawal by **mid-October** from most of India.
- The **Northeast Monsoon (Oct–Dec)** brings rain to Tamil Nadu and Andhra Pradesh.

**3. Spatial Distribution:**
- **West Coast & NE India:** Very high (>2000 mm/season) due to orographic lift.
- **Central India:** Moderate (750–1500 mm).
- **NW India (Rajasthan, Kutch):** Low (<300 mm).
- **Southern tip of India:** Both branches — moderate.
- **LEEWARD sides of Western Ghats** (rain shadow): Low rainfall.

**4. Inter-annual Variability:**
- All-India **CV ≈ 10%**; sub-division CV ≈ 20–40%.
- Linked to:
  - **ENSO** (correlation ≈ −0.5 with ISMR).
  - **IOD** (correlation ≈ +0.4 with ISMR).
  - **Eurasian snow cover** (negative correlation — heavy snow delays monsoon).
  - **Himalayan heating** (Tibet plateau sensible heat flux — May).

**5. Role of ENSO:**
- **El Niño (warm phase):** Eastward shift of Walker ascending branch, **suppressed convection** over Indian Ocean → **weakened ISMR** (e.g., 2002, 2009, 2015).
- **La Niña (cold phase):** Enhanced Walker cell over Indian Ocean → **stronger ISMR** (e.g., 2010, 2011).

**6. Role of IOD:**
- **Positive IOD (DMI > 0.4 °C):** Warm west Indian Ocean, cool east → enhanced convection over India → **stronger ISMR**; can offset El Niño (e.g., 1997 was a strong El Niño but positive IOD produced near-normal ISMR).
- **Negative IOD:** Cooler west, warmer east → suppressed convection over India → **weaker ISMR**.

**Conclusion:** ISMR is a **chaotic, multi-forced** system. Operational forecasting (IMD's Monsoon Mission) uses coupled dynamical models that ingest ENSO + IOD + snow cover + land surface conditions.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — Blaney-Criddle

**Given:**
- Latitude: 22°N
- Mean monthly temperature (August): $T = 29.0$ °C
- Mean daily % of annual daytime hours (August, 22°N): $p = 9.1$
- Crop coefficient: $K_c = 1.10$
- Days in August: $n = 31$
- Simplified formula (per the question): $ET_o \, (\text{mm}) = p \cdot T / 10$ for the **daily** equivalent.

**Step 1: Daily reference ET.**

$$
ET_o^{\text{daily}} = \frac{p \cdot T}{10} = \frac{9.1 \times 29.0}{10} = \frac{263.9}{10} = 26.39 \; \text{mm/day}
$$

**Step 2: Apply crop coefficient for paddy.**

$$
ET_c^{\text{daily}} = K_c \cdot ET_o^{\text{daily}} = 1.10 \times 26.39 = 29.03 \; \text{mm/day}
$$

**Step 3: Multiply by number of days (31) to get monthly value.**

$$
ET_c^{\text{monthly}} = 29.03 \times 31 = 900.0 \; \text{mm/month}
$$

**Final Answer:**
- Daily reference ET: **26.4 mm/day**
- Daily crop ET (paddy): **29.0 mm/day**
- Monthly crop ET (paddy): **900 mm/month**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Many students confuse $p$ (a *daily percentage*, dimensionless) with $T$ (a temperature). **The unit consistency of $p$ as a fraction or percent must be tracked carefully.** Some textbooks use $p$ as a *decimal* (e.g., 0.091) and others as a *percent* (e.g., 9.1). Always state the convention you are using. **Lose 1 mark** if the unit is not clarified.

---

### Question B (14 Marks, Internal Choice Alternative) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1, Alternate Set]

**Q:** *(a)* Explain the **Penman-Monteith (FAO-56) method** for estimating reference evapotranspiration (ET_o). Derive the simplified form of the equation and explain the physical meaning of the **surface resistance (r_s)** and **aerodynamic resistance (r_a)**. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* The following data are recorded for a station:
- Mean temperature $T = 28$ °C
- Relative humidity $RH = 55\%$
- Wind speed at 2 m, $u_2 = 2.5$ m/s
- Net radiation $R_n = 13$ MJ/m²/day
- Soil heat flux $G = 0.5$ MJ/m²/day
- Station pressure $P = 100$ kPa

Compute the **daily reference evapotranspiration (ET_o)** using the **FAO-56 Penman-Monteith** equation. Take the psychrometric constant $\gamma = 0.0667$ kPa/°C. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**The Penman-Monteith (FAO-56) Method:**

The Penman-Monteith equation integrates the **energy balance** with an **aerodynamic transport model** in which vapor is transported through two resistances in series: the **surface (canopy) resistance** $r_s$ and the **aerodynamic resistance** $r_a$.

**Derivation of the Simplified FAO-56 Form:**

**Step 1:** General Penman-Monteith equation:

$$
\lambda ET = \frac{\Delta (R_n - G) + \rho_a c_p (e_s - e_a) / r_a}{\Delta + \gamma (1 + r_s / r_a)}
$$

**Step 2:** For the **FAO-56 reference crop** (short grass, $h = 0.12$ m, $r_s = 70$ s/m, $z_{om} = 0.123 h$, $z_{ov} = 0.1 h$):
- $r_a = 208 / u_2$ (s/m, where $u_2$ is m/s at 2 m height)
- $r_s / r_a = 70 \cdot u_2 / 208 \approx 0.337 \cdot u_2 \approx 0.34 \cdot u_2$

**Step 3:** Substitute the numerical value of $\rho_a c_p / (\lambda \cdot 0.622) \approx 900$ K·mm·kPa·kg⁻¹·m³:

$$
ET_o = \frac{0.408 \cdot \Delta (R_n - G) + \gamma \cdot \dfrac{900}{T + 273} \cdot u_2 (e_s - e_a)}{\Delta + \gamma (1 + 0.34 \cdot u_2)}
$$

**Step 4:** The constant **0.408** is the conversion $1/\lambda$ in mm/MJ (since $\lambda = 2.45$ MJ/kg and 1 kg water covers 1 mm over 1 m²: $1/2.45 \approx 0.408$).

**Physical meaning of $r_s$ (surface resistance):**
- Represents the resistance of vapor flow through **stomata** (leaf pores), the **cuticle**, and the **soil surface** in series.
- For the reference crop: $r_s = 70$ s/m.
- It depends on **LAI, stomatal conductance, soil moisture, and solar radiation**.
- High $r_s$ → stomata closed → transpiration suppressed (night, drought stress).

**Physical meaning of $r_a$ (aerodynamic resistance):**
- Represents the resistance to vapor transfer from the evaporating surface to the **measurement height** (2 m for $u_2$).
- Depends on **wind speed** and **surface roughness** ($z_{om}$).
- $r_a = 208 / u_2$ — higher wind → lower resistance → more vapor transport.
- For very rough surfaces (forests), $r_a$ is much lower.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — Numerical

**Given:**
- $T = 28$ °C
- $RH = 55\%$
- $u_2 = 2.5$ m/s
- $R_n = 13$ MJ/m²/day
- $G = 0.5$ MJ/m²/day
- $P = 100$ kPa
- $\gamma = 0.0667$ kPa/°C

**Step 1: Saturation vapor pressure $e_s(T)$ at 28 °C.**

$$
e_s(28) = 0.6108 \cdot \exp\left(\frac{17.27 \times 28}{28 + 237.3}\right) = 0.6108 \cdot \exp\left(\frac{483.56}{265.3}\right)
$$

$$
= 0.6108 \cdot \exp(1.823) = 0.6108 \times 6.194 = 3.784 \; \text{kPa}
$$

**Step 2: Actual vapor pressure $e_a$.**

$$
e_a = e_s \cdot \frac{RH}{100} = 3.784 \times 0.55 = 2.081 \; \text{kPa}
$$

**Step 3: Vapor pressure deficit VPD.**

$$
e_s - e_a = 3.784 - 2.081 = 1.703 \; \text{kPa}
$$

**Step 4: Slope of saturation curve $\Delta$ at 28 °C.**

$$
\Delta = \frac{4098 \times e_s(T)}{(T + 237.3)^2} = \frac{4098 \times 3.784}{(28 + 237.3)^2} = \frac{15507}{(265.3)^2} = \frac{15507}{70384} = 0.2203 \; \text{kPa/°C}
$$

**Step 5: Numerator.**

**Radiation term:**

$$
N_{\text{rad}} = 0.408 \times \Delta \times (R_n - G) = 0.408 \times 0.2203 \times (13 - 0.5)
$$

$$
= 0.408 \times 0.2203 \times 12.5 = 0.408 \times 2.754 = 1.124 \; \text{mm/day equivalent}
$$

**Aerodynamic term:**

$$
N_{\text{aero}} = \gamma \times \frac{900}{T + 273} \times u_2 \times (e_s - e_a)
$$

$$
= 0.0667 \times \frac{900}{301} \times 2.5 \times 1.703
$$

$$
= 0.0667 \times 2.99 \times 2.5 \times 1.703
$$

$$
= 0.0667 \times 12.73 = 0.849 \; \text{mm/day equivalent}
$$

**Total Numerator:**

$$
N = 1.124 + 0.849 = 1.973 \; \text{mm/day}
$$

**Step 6: Denominator.**

$$
D = \Delta + \gamma (1 + 0.34 \cdot u_2) = 0.2203 + 0.0667 \times (1 + 0.34 \times 2.5)
$$

$$
= 0.2203 + 0.0667 \times 1.85 = 0.2203 + 0.1234 = 0.3437
$$

**Step 7: Final ET_o.**

$$
ET_o = \frac{N}{D} = \frac{1.973}{0.3437} = 5.74 \; \text{mm/day}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** $ET_o \approx 5.74$ mm/day for the reference crop. This is a typical value for a humid tropical/subtropical pre-monsoon or monsoon-onset day.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning (Numerical):**
> 1. **Forgetting the $G$ term:** The numerator uses $(R_n - G)$, not $R_n$ alone. $G$ is non-trivial in May and October in India (typically 1–2 MJ/m²/day in the daytime). **Lose 0.5–1 mark.**
> 2. **Mixing $T$ units:** The $\dfrac{900}{T + 273}$ term uses $T$ in °C, then **adds 273 to convert to Kelvin** (K). Forgetting the +273 will give a wildly wrong aerodynamic term. **Lose 1 mark.**
> 3. **Pressure adjustment to $\gamma$:** If $P \neq 101.3$ kPa, recompute $\gamma$. Here we are *given* $\gamma = 0.0667$ for $P = 100$ kPa (slightly lower than standard).
> 4. **Common-sense check:** ET_o > 8 mm/day is rare in India except over arid Rajasthan in May. ET_o < 2 mm/day is rare except in winter. **5.74 mm/day is plausible.**

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 1 — Monsoon, Climate Oscillations, ET Methods)**

### Monsoon Essentials
- **Indian Summer Monsoon Rainfall (ISMR)** = June–September rainfall, ~75% of annual total.
- **Onset** over Kerala: ~1 June (Kokatatmy criterion).
- **Withdrawal**: begins early September from NW, full retreat by mid-October.
- **Spatial CV** is high; **temporal CV** all-India ~10%.
- **Active (3–4 day wet)** and **Break (5–7 day dry)** spells alternate.

### Climate Oscillations
- **ENSO**: El Niño (warm) → **weakens** ISMR (corr ≈ −0.5). La Niña → **strengthens** ISMR.
- **IOD (DMI)**: Positive IOD (>+0.4 °C) → **strengthens** ISMR; can offset El Niño.
- **MJO**: Modulates sub-seasonal active/break spells.
- **NAO & AMO**: Influence winter precipitation and multi-decadal trends.

### Evapotranspiration — Key Methods
- **Blaney-Criddle**: Temperature-based empirical; $u = pT/100$; needs only T and latitude.
- **Penman**: Combines energy balance + aerodynamic; needs T, RH, u, $R_n$; $E_p$ originally for open water.
- **Penman-Monteith (FAO-56)**: Global standard; explicitly includes $r_s = 70$ s/m and $r_a = 208/u_2$.

### Formula Quick-Reference (memorize verbatim)
- **$e_s(T) = 0.6108 \cdot \exp[17.27 T / (T + 237.3)]$** (kPa)
- **$\Delta = 4098 \cdot e_s(T) / (T + 237.3)^2$** (kPa/°C)
- **$\gamma = c_p P / (0.622 \lambda)$** (kPa/°C) ≈ 0.0667 at $P = 100$ kPa
- **$ET_o = \dfrac{0.408 \cdot \Delta \cdot (R_n - G) + \gamma \cdot \frac{900}{T + 273} \cdot u_2 \cdot (e_s - e_a)}{\Delta + \gamma (1 + 0.34 u_2)}$** (mm/day)

### Engineering Applications
- **Irrigation water requirement** = $K_c \cdot ET_o$ for the crop growth stage.
- **Reservoir yield design** uses mean monthly ET from Penman-Monteith.
- **Climate change impact** on ISMR (IPCC AR6: monsoons intensifying in extreme events, weakening in mean).

### KTU-Style Mark Distribution (Typical)
- **Conceptual Monsoon questions (Part A)**: 3–4 marks for definition + examples.
- **Numerical ET (Part B)**: 7 marks split between formula recall (2) + substitution (3) + final answer (1) + units (1).
- **Internal choice** in Part B: one Blaney-Criddle OR one Penman-Monteith numerical.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Tip:** If you remember only **one equation** for the entire module, remember the **FAO-56 Penman-Monteith** equation. It is worth 6–8 marks by itself and forms the basis for crop coefficient problems in Module 2.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Causes and Effects of Climate Change, Modeling of Hydrologic Impact of Climate Change on Water Resources - Typical Framework, General Circulation Models and Regional Climate Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET526 - APPLIED HYDROLOGY AND CLIMATOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Causes and Effects of Climate Change, Modeling of Hydrologic Impact of Climate Change on Water Resources - Typical Framework, General Circulation Models and Regional Climate Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Climate Change — Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Climate Change** is defined by the **Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC)** as *"a change in the state of the climate that can be identified by changes in the mean and/or the variability of its properties and that persists for an extended period, typically decades or longer."* It refers to systematic, long-term shifts in temperature, precipitation, wind patterns, and other climatic variables attributable to **natural internal processes** and/or **persistent anthropogenic modifications** to the atmospheric composition and land surface.

Climate differs fundamentally from **weather**: weather describes atmospheric conditions over short timescales (hours to weeks), while climate represents the **statistical aggregate** of weather patterns over a reference period — conventionally **30 years** as defined by the **World Meteorological Organization (WMO)**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Blanket Around the Earth" 🌍

Imagine the Earth wrapped in a thin **cotton blanket** (the atmosphere). Sunlight passes through and warms the surface, which then radiates heat back outward as infrared energy. The blanket traps some of this outgoing heat — this is the natural **greenhouse effect**, which keeps Earth habitable (average **+15 °C** instead of a freezing **−18 °C**).

Now imagine we keep **doubling the thickness of that blanket** by pumping more carbon dioxide, methane, and other gases into the air. The surface gets warmer, weather patterns shift, glaciers melt, and the water cycle intensifies. That is **enhanced greenhouse-driven climate change**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Greenhouse Gases (GHGs):** CO₂ (**~60%** of forcing), CH₄ (**~20%**), N₂O (**~6%**), Halocarbons/CFCs (**~14%**). Water vapor is the most abundant GHG but is treated as a **feedback**, not a forcing, because its concentration is controlled by temperature, not directly by human emissions.

## 1.3 Two Broad Categories of Causes

| Category | Examples | Timescale |
|----------|----------|-----------|
| **Natural Causes** | Solar irradiance variation, volcanic aerosols, Milankovitch orbital cycles, ocean–atmosphere oscillations (ENSO, NAO, AMO, PDO, IOD) | Decadal to **100,000-year** cycles |
| **Anthropogenic (Human-Induced) Causes** | Fossil fuel combustion, deforestation, industrial agriculture, urbanization, aerosol emissions, cement production, refrigerant leakage | Rapid — **decades** |

## 1.4 Effects of Climate Change (Engineering-Relevant)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Direct effects** (1st-order): Global mean surface temperature rise, sea-level rise, glacier retreat, ocean warming & acidification.
>
> **Hydrological effects** (2nd-order, KTU-focus): Altered precipitation, intensified hydrological cycle, shifts in runoff regimes, increased flood & drought frequency, reduced snowpack & altered ET.

A commonly cited benchmark is the **Paris Agreement** target of limiting warming to **1.5 °C above pre-industrial (1850–1900)** levels.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Causes of Climate Change

### A. Natural Causes

1. **Solar Irradiance Variability**
   - The Sun's output varies over the **11-year sunspot cycle** (~1–1.5 W m⁻² variation in Total Solar Irradiance, TSI).
   - Cannot explain the rapid warming of the past **50 years**, as TSI has been *flat or declining* while temperatures rose.

2. **Volcanic Eruptions**
   - Eject **stratospheric sulfate aerosols** (SO₂ → H₂SO₄ droplets) that reflect sunlight.
   - Cause **short-term cooling** (1–3 years), e.g., Mt. Pinatubo (1991) caused ~**0.5 °C** global cooling.

3. **Milankovitch Cycles (Orbital Forcing)**
   - Eccentricity (~100,000 yr), obliquity (~41,000 yr), precession (~23,000 yr).
   - Control long-term ice-age cycles — too slow to explain recent warming.

4. **Ocean–Atmosphere Oscillations**
   - **ENSO** (El Niño–Southern Oscillation), **NAO**, **AMO**, **PDO**, **IOD** — redistribute heat regionally on interannual–decadal scales.

### B. Anthropogenic Causes

1. **Greenhouse Gas Emissions**
   - CO₂ from fossil fuel combustion + cement: ~**36 GtCO₂/yr (2022)**.
   - Atmospheric CO₂ has risen from pre-industrial **~280 ppm** to **~422 ppm (2024)** — a **~50% increase**.

2. **Land Use Change**
   - Deforestation reduces carbon sinks and lowers surface albedo.
   - Urban heat islands intensify local warming.

3. **Aerosols (Short-Lived Climate Forcers, SLCFs)**
   - Sulfate, black carbon, organic carbon aerosols — exert both cooling (scattering) and warming (absorption) effects.

## 2.2 Key Physical Laws (Foundation of Climate Models)

### 2.2.1 Stefan–Boltzmann Law

$$E = \sigma \, T^{4}$$

Where:
- $E$ = emitted radiant exitance (W m⁻²)
- $\sigma$ = **Stefan–Boltzmann constant = 5.670 × 10⁻⁸ W m⁻² K⁻⁴**
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)

The Earth, with mean surface temperature $T_s \approx 288$ K, radiates:

$$E = (5.670 \times 10^{-8}) (288)^{4} \approx 390 \; \text{W m}^{-2}$$

### 2.2.2 Planetary Energy Balance

$$(1 - \alpha) \, S_{0}/4 = \sigma \, T_{e}^{4}$$

Where:
- $\alpha$ = planetary albedo ≈ **0.30**
- $S_{0}$ = solar constant ≈ **1361 W m⁻²**
- $T_{e}$ = effective radiating temperature of Earth

Solving: $T_e \approx 255$ K (≈ −18 °C) — confirming the **34 °C greenhouse warming**.

### 2.2.3 Radiative Forcing

$$\Delta F = \frac{\Delta S}{4(1 - \alpha/2)}$$

For a layer of optical thickness $\tau$ in the atmosphere, an **instantaneous radiative forcing** from a doubling of CO₂ is approximately:

$$\Delta F_{2\times CO_{2}} \approx 3.7 \; \text{W m}^{-2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This **3.7 W m⁻²** is the canonical IPCC benchmark used in **Climate Sensitivity** calculations.

### 2.2.4 Climate Sensitivity (Equilibrium)

$$\Delta T_{2\times} = \lambda \, \Delta F_{2\times}$$

Where:
- $\lambda$ = climate sensitivity parameter (K per W m⁻²), typically **0.5 – 1.0 K/(W m⁻²)**
- Best estimate of $\Delta T_{2\times}$: **2.5 – 4.0 °C** (likely range)

### 2.2.5 Clausius–Clapeyron Relation (Water Vapor Feedback)

$$\frac{d e_{s}}{dT} = \frac{L \, e_{s}}{R_{v} \, T^{2}}$$

Atmospheric moisture-holding capacity rises by **~7% per °C** of warming — the **thermodynamic foundation** for intensified hydrological extremes.

### 2.2.6 Water Balance under Climate Change

$$P = ET + R + \Delta S + G$$

Where $P$ = precipitation, $ET$ = evapotranspiration, $R$ = runoff, $\Delta S$ = storage change, $G$ = groundwater recharge. Climate change perturbs each term non-uniformly.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Equation / Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---------------------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Stefan–Boltzmann Emission | $E = \sigma T^{4}$ | W m⁻² | $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ |
| 2 | Solar constant mean flux | $S_{avg} = S_{0}/4 = 340$ | W m⁻² | At top-of-atmosphere average |
| 3 | Effective radiating T | $T_e = [S_0(1-\alpha)/4\sigma]^{1/4} = 255$ | K | Greenhouse-adjusted: $T_s = 288$ K |
| 4 | Radiative forcing (doubled CO₂) | $\Delta F = 3.7$ | W m⁻² | IPCC canonical value |
| 5 | Equilibrium temp. change | $\Delta T = \lambda \cdot \Delta F$ | K | $\lambda \approx 0.5 - 1.0$ |
| 6 | Clausius–Clapeyron scaling | $\Delta e_s \approx 7\% \cdot \Delta T$ | % per K | Hydrological intensification |
| 7 | Water balance | $P - ET = R + \Delta S$ | mm/yr | Climate-perturbed |
| 8 | Spatial scaling (grid ratio) | $A = N \cdot r^{2}$ | km² | $N$ = grid cells, $r$ = resolution |
| 9 | Penman–Monteith ET | $ET_0$ (FAO-56) | mm/day | Standard hydrology model |
| 10 | Wet-bulb temperature limit | $T_{wb,max} = 31$ | °C | Human survivability threshold |

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

- **Water Resources Engineering:** Quantifying altered design floods, reservoir yield, irrigation demand.
- **Hydropower:** Inflow forecasting under changing snowmelt regimes.
- **Urban Drainage:** Re-designing stormwater systems for intensified rainfall.
- **Coastal Engineering:** Sea-level rise adaptation, revised still-water levels.
- **Agricultural Engineering:** Revised crop water requirements, drought planning.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Global Mean Energy Balance & Greenhouse Effect

**Step 1: Incoming solar radiation intercepted by Earth (geometric factor).**

A sphere of cross-sectional area $\pi R^2$ intercepts sunlight, but the energy is distributed over the full surface $4\pi R^2$. Hence the average insolation per unit area is:

$$S_{in} = \frac{S_0}{4} = \frac{1361}{4} = 340.25 \; \text{W m}^{-2}$$

**Step 2: Account for planetary albedo $\alpha$ (reflection by clouds, ice, aerosols).**

The absorbed shortwave radiation is:

$$S_{abs} = (1 - \alpha) \frac{S_0}{4}$$

With $\alpha = 0.30$:

$$S_{abs} = (0.70)(340.25) = 238.18 \; \text{W m}^{-2}$$

**Step 3: Equate absorbed shortwave with outgoing longwave radiation (OLR) at radiative equilibrium.**

By the Stefan–Boltzmann law, the Earth radiates as a blackbody at effective temperature $T_e$:

$$\sigma \, T_e^{4} = (1 - \alpha) \frac{S_0}{4}$$

**Step 4: Solve for the effective radiating temperature.**

$$T_e = \left[ \frac{(1 - \alpha) S_0}{4 \sigma} \right]^{1/4}$$

Substituting numerical values:

$$T_e = \left[ \frac{(0.70)(1361)}{4 \times 5.670 \times 10^{-8}} \right]^{1/4} = [4.20 \times 10^{9}]^{1/4} \approx 254.6 \; \text{K}$$

**Step 5: Compare with observed mean surface temperature $T_s \approx 288$ K.**

$$\Delta T_{GH} = T_s - T_e = 288 - 254.6 \approx 33.4 \; \text{K}$$

This **~34 K** difference is the natural greenhouse effect, attributable to atmospheric water vapor (~60%), CO₂ (~22%), clouds, and other GHGs.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Climate Sensitivity to a Doubling of CO₂

**Step 1: Start from radiative forcing (IPCC benchmark).**

$$\Delta F_{2\times CO_2} = 3.7 \; \text{W m}^{-2}$$

**Step 2: Apply Planck feedback $\lambda_P$ (negative feedback that stabilizes the system).**

$$\lambda_P = -\frac{1}{T_e} \cdot \frac{\partial E}{\partial T} = -\frac{4 \sigma T_e^{3}}{T_e \cdot (1 - \alpha)S_0/4} \approx -3.2 \; \text{W m}^{-2} \text{K}^{-1}$$

**Step 3: Sum feedbacks (Planck + water vapor + lapse rate + surface albedo + cloud).**

$$\lambda_{total} = \lambda_P + \lambda_{WV} + \lambda_{LR} + \lambda_{alb} + \lambda_{cld}$$

Best-estimate total feedback parameter:

$$\lambda_{total} \approx -1.0 \; \text{W m}^{-2} \text{K}^{-1}$$

**Step 4: Solve for equilibrium temperature change.**

$$\Delta T_{eq} = -\frac{\Delta F_{2\times CO_2}}{\lambda_{total}} = -\frac{3.7}{-1.0} = 3.7 \; \text{K}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **likely range (66% confidence)** is **2.5 – 4.0 °C** per CO₂ doubling. The transient response (before deep-ocean equilibration) is roughly **half** this value, hence the term *Transient Climate Response (TCR)*.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Clausius–Clapeyron & Hydrological Intensification

**Step 1: Start from the Clausius–Clapeyron differential form.**

$$\frac{1}{e_s} \frac{de_s}{dT} = \frac{L}{R_v T^{2}}$$

**Step 2: Insert numerical values at $T_0 = 288$ K.**

- $L$ = latent heat of vaporization ≈ **2.5 × 10⁶ J kg⁻¹**
- $R_v$ = specific gas constant for water vapor ≈ **461 J kg⁻¹ K⁻¹**

$$\frac{1}{e_s} \frac{de_s}{dT} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{6}}{461 \times (288)^{2}} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{6}}{3.82 \times 10^{7}} \approx 0.065 \; \text{K}^{-1}$$

**Step 3: Interpret as a percentage.**

$$\frac{de_s}{dT} \approx 6.5 - 7.0\% \; \text{per K}$$

This explains why a **1 °C** warming can intensify the most extreme precipitation events by **~7%** (and even more in convective tropics, "super-CC scaling").

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Scale-Aware Spatial Resolution Comparison

Let the GCM have resolution $r_G$ and the RCM have resolution $r_R$. The number of grid points over a basin of area $A$ scales as:

$$N_G = \frac{A}{r_G^{2}}, \quad N_R = \frac{A}{r_R^{2}}$$

**Step 5: Compute ratio for $A = 10{,}000 \; \text{km}^{2}$, $r_G = 150$ km, $r_R = 25$ km.**

$$N_G = \frac{10{,}000}{(150)^{2}} \approx 0.44 \; \text{(insufficient)}$$

$$N_R = \frac{10{,}000}{(25)^{2}} = 16 \; \text{grid cells}$$

This demonstrates why **downscaling is essential** for catchment-scale hydrology.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — GCM vs. RCM vs. Statistical Downscaling

| Criterion | GCM | RCM | Statistical Downscaling |
|-----------|-----|-----|--------------------------|
| Spatial Resolution | 100–250 km | 10–50 km | 1–10 km (station scale) |
| Computational Cost | Very High (PFlops) | High (TFlops) | Low |
| Physical Consistency | Yes | Yes | No (empirical) |
| Bias Correction Needed | Yes | Yes | Implicit |
| Domain | Global | Regional | Station-specific |
| Typical Use | Continental / global drivers | Catchment-scale studies | Local impact assessment |
| Key Examples | HadGEM3, GFDL-CM4, EC-Earth3 | RegCM4, PRECIS, RACMO | BCSD, BCCA, SDSM, LARS-WG |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
